scriptFull:
By the time the breach became public in late 2024, US officials confirmed nine major American carriers had been compromised. The Big Three — AT&T, Verizon, and Lumen Technologies — plus six regional and infrastructure providers including Charter Communications, Windstream, Consolidated Communications, Spectrum, and Viasat. Between them, these nine carriers handle the communications of the overwhelming majority of Americans.
But this was not simply an American story. By August 2025, the FBI confirmed that Salt Typhoon had hacked at least 200 companies across 80 countries, and the geographic scope — assembled from reporting by TechCrunch, Trend Micro, and multiple government advisories — covers virtually the entire western world. Canada confirmed breaches at its top telecommunications firms. Australia's ASIO director-general publicly confirmed attempts to access critical infrastructure. The UK confirmed a cluster of activity across its telecommunications sector. Singapore confirmed that all four of its major telecoms had been hit, triggering an eleven-month cleanup operation. Across the Five Eyes intelligence alliance and well beyond — South America, Southeast Asia, the Indo-Pacific, Europe — the pattern was the same at every entry point: Cisco routers at the network edge, and behind them the CALEA-style lawful intercept systems that virtually every western government had legally required its telecoms to maintain.
And it did not stop with telecommunications. In December 2025, intrusions were detected in several United States House of Representatives committees and subsequently attributed to Salt Typhoon — meaning that by the end of 2025, the group had moved from wiretapping America's phones to sitting inside America's legislative branch.
Because CALEA is not uniquely American — the UK's Investigatory Powers Act imposes the same capabilities, as do similar laws across much of the developed world — Salt Typhoon did not find one backdoor. They found an entire internationally mandated category of backdoor and worked their way through it systematically, country by country, carrier by carrier.
The Electronic Frontier Foundation, which had opposed CALEA since the day it passed in 1994, published a single sentence in response to the breach that captured three decades of ignored warnings: "There is no backdoor that only lets in good guys and keeps out bad guys."
